title: Petticoat tails
subtitle: Scottish shortbread cut into fan wedges
excerpt: Scottish shortbread baked as a large round disc and cut while warm into eight triangular wedges. The border is crimped and the centre is pricked before baking. One disc makes eight pieces.
sourceNotes: Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): petticoat tails are recorded as the traditional Scottish presentation of shortbread — a large round disc marked into triangular wedges, named for the bell-shaped hooped petticoats of the sixteenth century. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: shortbread baked as a round with crimped edges and scored into sections is documented as the classic Edinburgh style, distinct from the finger biscuit of Aberdeenshire.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the baked disc whole, wrapped in baking paper, for up to 3 months.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in an airtight tin for 2 weeks. Flavour deepens after the first day.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf; pale gold, not brown — a thick disc takes longer than individual rounds so a lower temperature is needed

content.text: Petticoat tails are the original shortbread format: one large round, crimped at the edge, scored into triangular wedges before baking, broken apart after. The same dough as shortbread rounds but baked lower and slower to cook the thick disc through without browning the edge.

Beat the {{unsalted-butter}}, {{caster-sugar}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} until pale and smooth. Work in the {{plain-flour}} until a smooth dough forms. Press into a flat disc, wrap, and refrigerate for 30 minutes.

Preheat the oven to 150°C fan. Line a baking tray with baking paper. Place the chilled dough on the lined tray and press or roll it into a round disc about 22 cm across and 1 cm thick. Crimp the edge between your thumb and forefinger all the way round. Use a small sharp knife to score eight equal wedges from the centre out, cutting three-quarters of the way through without separating the disc. Prick the surface all over with a fork to prevent blistering. Sprinkle a little {{caster-sugar}} over the top if you like.

Bake for 23–27 minutes until the disc is pale gold across the top and the edge is set firm. Remove from the oven and immediately re-score along the original lines, this time cutting all the way through while the shortbread is still soft. Leave on the tray for 5 minutes to firm, then slide onto a rack. The wedges will hold together until broken by hand.
